ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general roti/whole wheat flatbread entry; no specific tandoori butter roti entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific tandoori butter roti code. A0CKP (roti/chapati) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for tandoori butter roti. No suitable parent code identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium tandoori butter roti weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served at a North Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Roti (Chapati), whole wheat, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), adapted with butter addition
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values for whole wheat roti (chapati) taken from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). Butter contribution (~5 g butter per 100 g roti, i.e., approximately 6.25 g butter per 80 g serving) added using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for butter (FDC ID 173410) to account for the butter topping. Energy, fat, and saturated fat adjusted accordingly. Sodium estimated from ICMR-NIN base plus minor salt contribution.

gi_source.title: Chapati, whole wheat flour — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for tandoori butter roti. GI of 62 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole wheat chapati/roti (GI ~62, medium category). The addition of butter slightly lowers the effective GI due to fat slowing gastric emptying; however, the published GI for whole wheat roti is retained as the primary reference. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 41.6) / 100 ≈ 25.8, where carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 52.0 × 0.80 = 41.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) and butter addition
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one roti and request minimal or no butter topping, as the butter addition contributes additional sodium and saturated fat that can elevate blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 62) and moderate glycemic load (~25.8 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume tandoori butter roti in controlled portions (one roti per meal) and pair it with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt the postprandial glycaemic response, given its medium GI and moderate glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~3.8 g/100 g) and dietary fibre benefit from whole wheat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat contributed by the butter topping; however, the whole wheat base provides dietary fibre (3.8 g/100 g) which supports LDL cholesterol management, and one roti consumed without excess butter is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Butter Roti is a traditional North Indian whole wheat flatbread (roti) that is prepared by baking a hand-rolled dough disc in a tandoor — a cylindrical clay oven fired with charcoal or wood — at very high temperatures, typically exceeding 400°C. The intense dry heat of the tandoor produces a bread with a slightly charred, blistered exterior and a soft, chewy interior. Immediately after baking, the roti is brushed or topped with a generous knob of butter, which melts into the surface and imparts a rich, savoury flavour and a glossy sheen. The dough is made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a small amount of salt, making it a minimally processed, fibre-rich staple. Compared to naan, tandoori roti is thinner, less calorie-dense, and does not contain leavening agents such as yeast or baking powder. In Singapore, tandoori butter roti is commonly served at North Indian restaurants as an accompaniment to curries, dals, and grilled meats. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~316 kcal/100 g), useful dietary fibre (~3.8 g/100 g) from the whole wheat base, and a moderate glycaemic index of approximately 62. The butter topping adds saturated fat and calories, making portion control relevant for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, hypertension, or Type 2 Diabetes. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori butter roti?
What is the difference between tandoori roti and naan?
Is tandoori butter roti good for diabetics?
What is the GI of tandoori roti?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for tandoori butter roti exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN whole wheat roti (chapati) entry with a calculated butter addition using USDA SR Legacy data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole wheat chapati. The adaptation is well-supported and methodologically sound, but the absence of a direct source entry limits confidence to medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for whole wheat roti/chapati, with butter contribution calculated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173410, butter, salted) at an estimated 5 g butter per 100 g roti. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category; butter contributes negligible vitamin D at the quantities used. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for whole wheat roti. 4. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; whole wheat roti contains no ascorbic acid. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (whole wheat chapati); no direct tandoori butter roti GI entry exists in any approved source. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (62 × 41.6) / 100 = 25.8, based on an 80 g serving containing 41.6 g carbohydrate. 7. This dish is served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore including Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine (South Bridge Road) and Bombay Grill (Restaurant Delivery) per field survey data (preserved from input, not used as nutrient evidence). 8. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302397 is used as closest parent for roti/whole wheat flatbread; no exact FoodOn entry for tandoori butter roti exists. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest parent for roti/chapati category.

Sourced GI from University of Sydney GI Database (whole wheat chapati analogous estimate) and calculated GL per 80 g serving.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented vitamin_c_mg = 0.0 as confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN.
Used only closest_parent match types for FoodOn and FoodEx2 with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none.
Ensured all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Included all three required health conditions with specific clinical guidance.
